data.title: Apple discontinues 256GB Mac mini; starting price climbs to $799
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) has stopped offering the M4 Mac mini with the 256GB storage option, which effectively increased the starting price to $799 from $599.

The 512GB Mac Mini with an M4 chip and 16GB of RAM, which was already priced at $799, now stands as the starting version of the device on the Apple site.

Apple CEO Tim Cook said customer demand for the Mac mini and Mac Studio has been stronger than expected and has led to supply constraints.

"We think, looking forward, that the Mini and the Mac Studio may take several months to reach supply-demand balance," Cook said during Apple's earnings call on Thursday night. 

"The primary constraint is the availability of the advanced nodes our SoCs are produced on, not memory," Cook said. "And so I don't want to predict our ability for supply and demand to match because if I look at it realistically, I think on the Mac Mini and the Max Studio, I believe it will take several months to reach supply-demand balance. And so we're not at the point where we're saying this is going to end anytime soon."

Apple plans to begin producing the Mac mini in the U.S. later this year.

"As part of our $600B commitment to the U.S., we were pleased to share recently that Mac mini production is coming to America later this year, expanding our factory operations in Houston with a brand-new facility," Cook said. "In March, we were thrilled to welcome four new companies to our American manufacturing program to help manufacture essential materials and components for Apple products sold worldwide."

The Mac mini has not only proven to be popular in the U.S. market but also abroad, as it proved to be the top-selling desktop in China during the March quarter, Cook said.

data.title: DiamondRock raises 2026 RevPAR outlook to 1.5%-3.5% and guides $296M-$308M adjusted EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We are pleased to report that first quarter results exceeded our expectations,” said (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Briony Quinn), citing “comparable RevPAR increased 2%,” “corporate adjusted EBITDA of $60.6 million,” and “adjusted FFO per share of $0.22,” while noting the quarter “faced disruptive weather challenges in several markets.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) described mix and spend trends, including “our resorts outperformed our urban hotels,” “group revenues were down 0.8%,” and “total RevPAR grew 2.5%,” adding that “out-of-room revenue per occupied room climbed 4%” and that resort out-of-room spend “averaged $320 per night.”

  • (CEO & Director Jeffrey Donnelly) highlighted governance and strategy updates: “we celebrated Bill McCarten as he retired… as Chairman,” and “welcomed Bruce Wardinski, to his first board meeting as our new Chairman,” while reiterating DiamondRock 2.0 actions that produced “a leaner G&A structure with a headcount per hotel ratio that remains about 50% below the peer average.”

  • (CEO & Director Donnelly) detailed a key asset decision at Westin Boston Seaport District, saying “the existing franchise agreement… expires on December 31, 2026,” and that the company chose “reinforcing the Westin brand’s superior position,” adding, “we elected not to pursue a key money loan.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) said the company is “under contract to sell one Hotel” with closing expected “during the second quarter,” and that proceeds “could include opportunistic share repurchases.”

Outlook

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) said, “We are raising our 2026 RevPAR guidance by 50 basis points to 1.5% to 3.5%,” with “adjusted EBITDA guidance… $296 million to $308 million,” and “adjusted FFO per share guidance… $1.12 to $1.18.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) attributed the raise to “stronger-than-expected first quarter operating performance” and “a more favorable renewal of our insurance program on April 1 than we had anticipated,” adding, “In aggregate over 3 years, we have reduced premiums by just under 40%.”

  • Compared with the prior call, management moved 2026 RevPAR from “1% to 3%” to “1.5% to 3.5%,” adjusted EBITDA from “$287 million to $302 million” to “$296 million to $308 million,” and adjusted FFO per share from “$1.09 to $1.16” to “$1.12 to $1.18.”

Financial results

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) said “comparable RevPAR increased 2% and total RevPAR increased 2.5%,” while “total operating expense growth” was “less than 1%,” and “our FFO margin increased… 225 basis points.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) reported segment performance: “transient outperformed with revenues up 2.1%,” while “group revenues were down 0.8%,” and in urban markets “RevPAR increased 0.9%.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) emphasized cost control: “total hotel operating expenses increased 0.8%,” “wages and benefits… increased just 0.7%,” and the portfolio delivered “a 127 basis point improvement in total EBITDA margins.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) noted capital and shareholder returns: “We paid a common dividend of $0.09 per share for the first quarter,” and “expect to declare quarterly dividends of $0.09 per share for the remainder of the year.”

Q&A

  • Jackson Armstrong, Wells Fargo: asked about capital deployment as shares rise; CEO Donnelly: “share repurchases are really the most appealing use.”

  • Jackson Armstrong, Wells Fargo: asked about labor/insurance/utilities drivers; President & COO Leonard: “we’ve been finding less hours worked… through productivity gains,” including “implementation of AI tools”; CEO Donnelly added insurance renewal savings “about $1 million benefit to the full year.”

  • Bennett Rose, Citi: asked on transaction markets and asset sale; CEO Donnelly: “transaction market today certainly feels a lot better than it did about a year ago,” with “interest rates… maybe 150 basis points lower,” while “pricing is still robust.”

  • Bennett Rose, Citi: asked on dividend payout ratio and NOL timing; CFO Quinn: “We’ve worked through about 50% of it… over the next few years.”

  • Michael Bellisario, Baird: asked about April/2Q and segment trajectory; CEO Donnelly: “the trajectory… in April continued to be healthy,” and “probability that all 3 channels… BT, leisure and group will be delivering positive growth.”

  • Michael Bellisario, Baird: asked why group pickup improved; President & COO Leonard: calendar shifts “gives us a larger number of potential pattern weeks that we can sell group into.”

  • Joshua Friedland, KeyBanc: asked how much group backfill is needed; President & COO Leonard referenced World Cup displacement; CEO Donnelly: “the actual magnitude… is… a single-digit millions of dollars.”

  • Joshua Friedland, KeyBanc: asked if asset sale is “testing the waters”; CEO Donnelly: “I wouldn’t call it necessarily testing the waters.”

  • Peter (for Duane), Evercore: asked on New York growth vs. margin pressure; President & COO Leonard: “there is going to be some margin pressure given the contract renewal,” but “continue to see demand… as strong as it’s been.”

  • Richard Hightower, Barclays: asked how $300+ ADR outperformance informs buy/sell/CapEx; President & COO Leonard said the trend is “something that we factor in acquisition decisions,” but assets “are being bid up”; CEO Donnelly said CapEx differs by true luxury and cited independence for “value engineer.”

  • Chris Darling, Green Street: asked about CapEx plan flexibility; CEO Donnelly: “don’t expect… that CapEx number is going to swing around a lot,” and “the intent… is to… derisk our future earnings volatility.”

  • Kenneth Billingsley, Compass Point: asked on World Cup’s 20 bps and booking behavior; CEO Donnelly: “there’s not much of a great precedent,” and ticket demand has been “about 1/3, 1/3, 1/3.”

  • Eduardo (for Floris), Ladenburg: asked about operator bonus accruals; President & COO Leonard: “we… track on an active basis,” and CEO Donnelly said accruing “mitigates risk.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on capital allocation, asset sales, labor, and event-driven demand; questions were direct but largely information-seeking (e.g., “best uses of incremental capital,” “updated thoughts on… pricing,” and “unpack… expectations for New York”).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, repeatedly emphasizing discipline and visibility (e.g., CEO Donnelly: “share repurchases… the most appealing,” and “don’t expect… CapEx number… to swing around a lot”).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management used more definitive language tied to a guide raise and specific cost benefit (CFO Quinn: “We are raising our 2026 RevPAR guidance”), while the prior call framed 2026 as “appropriate and measured” amid macro uncertainty.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call added a specific franchise-renewal conclusion for Westin Seaport (“reinforcing the Westin brand”) and clarified “we elected not to pursue a key money loan,” versus the prior call’s earlier-stage comment that the company had “not… finalized” the deal.

  • Analysts maintained similar focus on transaction markets, CapEx discipline, and event impacts (World Cup and July 4/America 250), while the current call placed more emphasis on near-term pacing (April, Memorial Day) and insurance savings.

  • Guidance language shifted from holding a measured range to explicitly raising ranges, with management tying the change to Q1 outperformance and insurance renewal.

Risks and concerns

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) cited demand and comparability headwinds: “tough quarter as we comped over our strongest revenue growth,” and “disruptive weather challenges.”

  • (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Quinn) flagged group softness drivers: “Winter storms in the Eastern U.S. and limited snow in our ski markets,” while stating, “We are encouraged by our hotels group pickup for the remainder of 2026.”

  • (CEO & Director Donnelly) noted external uncertainty in transactions: “recent geopolitical events have slowed the pace of some discussions,” and said asset sales will occur only when “recycling reduces risk or drives free cash flow per share growth.”

Final takeaway

Management said Q1 results came in ahead of expectations despite weather and difficult comparisons, driven by resort strength, resilient on-property spending, and disciplined expense control. The company raised full-year 2026 guidance ranges, attributing the increase to Q1 performance and a favorable insurance renewal, while reiterating a capital allocation posture that prioritizes share repurchases and ROI-driven projects. Management also highlighted a pending hotel sale expected to close in Q2, a completed board chair transition, and a Westin Boston Seaport franchise renewal decision intended to “minimize disruption” and support shareholder value starting in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Global economy faces crossroads in wake of energy shock - JPMorgan
data.description:
The global economy is at a crossroads as energy shocks stemming from the Middle East conflict create a tug-of-war between economic momentum and building price pressures, according to JPMorgan’s chief economist.

Bruce Kasman told CNBC on Friday that while the global economy shows healthy momentum heading through the first quarter, driven by strong tech demand across Asia and improving business sentiment, the situation remains precarious because of energy market disruptions.

“We’re starting to draw down inventories,” Kasman said. “We’re starting to potentially run into some physical constraints in places like Asia.”

The economist warned that if markets don’t believe the ongoing conflict will be resolved soon, there is a risk of a “scramble for oil” (USO) (BNO) that could trigger nonlinear price increases.

On monetary policy, Kasman said the Federal Reserve is taking the right approach by expressing patience amid two-sided risks of slower growth and higher inflation. He noted the Fed serves as an anchor for other central banks and equity markets (SPY) (VT).

However, Kasman cautioned that even in a constructive scenario, the Fed may face pressure to raise rates within six to nine months. He expects energy prices to remain elevated, inflation to persist around 3% and labor markets to tighten because of weak supply.

data.title: Summit hotel properties forecasts 0.5% to 3% 2026 RevPAR growth as World Cup pace trends up high teens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Summit Hotel Properties (INN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased with our first quarter financial results, which were driven by a meaningful sequential improvement in operating fundamentals throughout the quarter," said (President, CEO & Director Jonathan Stanner), highlighting that "RevPAR in our pro forma portfolio inflected positive in the first quarter, increasing 20 basis points year-over-year."

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) said March was the inflection point: "While RevPAR declined in January and February, those declines were more than offset by 4.1% RevPAR growth in March," adding it was "driven by a robust 5.6% increase in average rate" and that the company has "been pleased to see these trends continue in April."

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) framed the demand mix shift as higher-rated business returning: "Our best-performing demand segments were our highest rated segments, which allowed us to yield out a portion of lower-rated business," and he pointed to business transient improvement: "RevPAR growth increased 3% for the quarter, and 10% in March in our negotiated segment."

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) detailed capital recycling and shareholder returns, including a closed sale and an under-contract sale: "The hotel was sold for $12.3 million" (Hilton Garden Inn Longview, Texas) and "we entered into an agreement to sell our wholly owned Courtyard and Residence in Dallas, Arlington South hotels for a combined sale price of $19 million." He also said, "we repurchased 1.4 million common shares for an aggregate purchase price of $6 million," and "as of March 31, 2026, we had approximately $29 million of remaining capacity under the program."

  • "For the first quarter, adjusted EBITDA was $44.2 million, and adjusted FFO was $25.5 million or $0.21 per share," said (Executive VP & CFO William H. Conkling), attributing outperformance to "positive RevPAR trends and strong cost controls."

Outlook

  • "We have increased our RevPAR growth outlook to 0.5% to 3%," said (Executive VP & CFO Conkling), adding that this "translates to adjusted EBITDA of $170 million to $181 million, and adjusted FFO of $0.75 to $0.85 per share."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) said the margin framework embeds costs and taxes: "we expect full year 2026 hotel EBITDA margins to range from flat to down 75 basis points, which includes approximately 25 basis points of headwinds from higher property taxes," while also reiterating "nominal expense growth of approximately 3%."

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) tied near-term momentum to Q2 pacing and event demand: "We expect April RevPAR to increase approximately 3.5%," and "our second quarter revenue pace is currently trending approximately 4% ahead of the same time last year," with June "particularly strong" given "the 2026 FIFA World Cup" exposure.

Financial Results

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) said top-line performance turned slightly positive: "First quarter pro forma RevPAR increased 0.2% year-over-year, driven exclusively by growth in average daily rate," and "RevPAR index increased to 116% of fair share."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) called out market-level drivers, including San Francisco: "RevPAR increasing 27% in the quarter," and South Florida: "RevPAR growth exceeding 14%, driven by a 9% increase in average daily rate." He also said the repositioned Oceanside Fort Lauderdale Beach produced "first quarter revenue and EBITDA growth of 56% and 90%, respectively."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) emphasized ancillary revenue and cost control: "Non-rooms revenue increased 10% year-over-year," while "pro forma operating expenses increased 3.6% year-over-year" and "nominal costs declined 6% versus first quarter 2025" for contract labor.

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) updated financing, liquidity, and capital spending: "we fully repaid our $288 million, 1.5% convertible senior notes," and "we have no debt maturities until 2028." He also said, "the company expects pro rata capital expenditures to range from $55 million to $65 million."

Q&A

  • Austin Wurschmidt, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division asked about pace degradation and last year’s pattern; (President, CEO & Director Stanner) replied, "what we've seen over the last 30 to 60 days has really been an acceleration in the month for the month," and said June pace is "trending up high teens" and "would expect that to normalize."

  • Michael Bellisario, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division asked to separate business transient vs. leisure and quantify Mexico rebookings; (President, CEO & Director Stanner) said growth skewed to "premium-rated retail channels and our negotiated channels," added Mexico disruption "was a benefit predominantly in our South Florida and Scottsdale markets," and said it was "beneficial, but not something that I think is going to create some sort of onetime 1-month distortion."

  • Chris Woronka, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division asked about direct bookings and branded credit cards; (President, CEO & Director Stanner) said, "We have continued to grow our share of direct bookings. We're probably plus or minus 70% for the full portfolio," and "we're driving the majority of our business through those channels."

  • Logan Epstein, Wolfe Research, LLC asked about the government segment inflection and embedded expectations; (President, CEO & Director Stanner) said, "Government revenue for us was down about 12% year-over-year," "we actually saw March government-related revenue go up 3%," and Q2 government pace is "trending up kind of mid-single digits year-over-year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly negative, centered on booking pace durability, mix (rate vs. occupancy), government demand recovery, and potential one-time impacts (Mexico disruption, loyalty program changes).

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and similarly steady in Q&A, using firmer language around acceleration and guidance while acknowledging uncertainty, including (President, CEO & Director Stanner) noting that "macro and geopolitical uncertainty persists."

  • Versus last quarter, management messaging shifted from preparing for a difficult Q1 to emphasizing realized Q1 outperformance and a raised full-year framework, while analysts continued to probe visibility and pacing dynamics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) raised the full-year 2026 RevPAR outlook to "0.5% to 3%" from the prior quarter’s "0% to 3%" initial range, and lifted adjusted EBITDA to "$170 million to $181 million" from "$167 million to $181 million."

  • The margin guide tightened, with (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) now expecting margins "flat to down 75 basis points" versus the prior quarter’s "flat to down 100 basis points" range.

  • Q&A focus was consistent quarter to quarter (pace, mix, World Cup exposure, Hyatt programs), but this quarter’s exchanges emphasized that recent strength was "a little bit of a reversal from the trends that we saw last year," per (President, CEO & Director Stanner).

Risks and Concerns

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) cited ongoing external uncertainty: guidance was set to "acknowledg[e] that our most meaningful quarters are still ahead and macro and geopolitical uncertainty persists."

  • (President, CEO & Director Stanner) described demand disruptions that pressured early-quarter results, including "the lingering government shutdown," "highly publicized TSA wait times," "Winter Storm Fern," and "civil unrest in Minneapolis."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Conkling) flagged expense pressures and taxes within the outlook, including "higher property taxes" and continued wage and benefits impacts from "our strategic shift to internal staffing."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an operating inflection driven by rate-led improvement that culminated in a strong March and continued into April, alongside increased full-year guidance for RevPAR, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted FFO per share. Leadership also highlighted portfolio actions—asset sales, refinancing that leaves "no debt maturities until 2028," and continued buybacks—while positioning World Cup and other event calendars as key demand catalysts, tempered by explicit references to macro and geopolitical uncertainty and cost pressures embedded in the margin outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: MasTec forecasts 2026 revenue of $17.5B and EPS of $8.79 as backlog reaches $20.3B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MasTec, Inc. (MTZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Revenue for the quarter was $3.829 billion, up 34% year-over-year" (CEO Jose Mas) and "adjusted earnings per share was $1.39" alongside "backlog at quarter end was $20.3 billion, a $1.4 billion sequential increase and a new record level" (CEO Jose Mas).

  • "AI is driving a level of demand for fiber capacity, redundancy and low latency that we haven't seen before" and "we think that creates a multiyear opportunity measured in the tens of billions of dollars" (CEO Jose Mas), while "Utilities are spending heavily on transmission, system hardening, and reliability" (CEO Jose Mas).

  • "Our recent turnkey data center award is progressing very well" and "the demand for both the skill set that MasTec has developed in construction management, coupled with the capabilities we have in civil, power, telecom and maintenance provides us the opportunity to exponentially grow this part of our business" (CEO Jose Mas).

  • "Given our strong performance and momentum, we are increasing our full year guidance" (CEO Jose Mas): "We now expect revenue of $17.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $1.5 billion and earnings per share of $8.79" (CEO Jose Mas).

  • "We now expect to generate almost 45% of our full year EBITDA in the first half of 2026, implying markedly lower seasonality than our business has experienced historically" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco).

Outlook

  • "We are raising our full year guidance to reflect the first quarter beat and our improving outlook for the remainder of 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco): "We now expect revenue of $17.5 billion" and "adjusted EBITDA" of "$1.5 billion" with "adjusted EPS" of "$8.79" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Segment-level direction included "Power Delivery" revenue guidance "higher to approximately $4.8 billion" with "Full year EBITDA margins" that "remain on track to approach double digits" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco), and Clean Energy & Infrastructure "full year revenue guidance to approximately $6.7 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Prior-quarter guidance language shifted upward: "For 2026 full year, we expect revenue of $17 billion" and "Adjusted EPS is forecast to be $8.40" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco, Q4 2025) versus "$17.5 billion" and "$8.79" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco, Q1 2026).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA was $284 million" and "adjusted earnings per share was $1.39" (CEO Jose Mas) on "$3.829 billion" of revenue (CEO Jose Mas).

  • Communications: "revenue of $802 million" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco) with "EBITDA margins" "about 100 basis points below last year's first quarter, negatively impacted by costs to exit certain markets in our DIRECTV fulfillment business" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Power Delivery: "backlog to a new record of $6.2 billion" and "Regarding Greenlink, our client resolved the transmission permitting review earlier than anticipated, and we are now operating across the full contractual scope" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Pipeline: "generating $682 million of revenue" with "EBITDA margins of 21%" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco), while "we are currently taking a conservative view around second half project timing and productivity" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Cash and working capital: "We generated cash flow from operations of $99 million" and "DSOs increase to 72 days versus 65 days at year-end" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco), with "liquidity" of "approximately $1.8 billion" and "net leverage of 1.8x" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

Q&A

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital Securities: asked about "how pricing and/or contract terms are changing"; CEO Mas answered, "I think we're just at the beginning of seeing some of the improvements that we saw in '25 relative to backlog and repricing, and I think that will play through the balance of '26 and into '27."

  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citigroup: asked if growth is sustainable in 2027; CEO Mas said, "I can't remember a time where every business was just humming" and added, "quite frankly, I would only expect it to get better."

  • Ati Modak, Goldman Sachs: asked about lower seasonality; Executive VP & CFO Dimarco said, "a lot of it is just around project timing" and "being proactive and really working with our clients to try to promote opportunities for us to keep our crews and our equipment productive."

  • Justin Hauke, Baird: pressed on guidance conservatism; CEO Mas said, "we kind of pushed the beat in Q1 through the guide through the year, didn't necessarily reforecast the balance of the year" and "there's a lot of conservatism built into that."

  • Philip Shen, ROTH: asked about "tax equity pause" risk to renewables; CEO Mas said, "We feel really comfortable about our book of business for '27" and "I promise you that renewables are an incredibly important part of the story in the near to midterm."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on sustainability and conservatism, including "what's underpinning the conservatism" (Justin Hauke, Baird) and seeking clarity on financing-related risk (Philip Shen, ROTH).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, including "we are confident in our outlook for 2026" (CEO Jose Mas) and "we couldn't be more excited about the opportunities that are on the way" (CEO Mas).

  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s tone remained upbeat but included more explicit upside framing tied to Q1 execution and guide-raise: "Given our strong performance and momentum, we are increasing our full year guidance" (CEO Mas) compared with Q4’s "we are excited about our outlook for 2026 and beyond" (CEO Mas, Q4 2025).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased from "revenue of $17 billion" and "Adjusted EPS" of "$8.40" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco, Q4 2025) to "$17.5 billion" revenue and "$8.79" adjusted EPS (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco, Q1 2026).

  • Power Delivery permitting narrative improved: Q4 noted "lower-than-planned Greenlink project volumes due to permitting-related delays" (CEO Jose Mas, Q4 2025), while Q1 stated "resolved the transmission permitting review earlier than anticipated" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco).

  • Q&A emphasis in Q1 leaned toward guidance conservatism, data center scale, and pipeline 2027 ramp timing; Q4 leaned more toward explaining the new data center general contractor win and segment margin trajectories.

Risks and Concerns

  • Working capital and collections: "DSOs increase to 72 days versus 65 days at year-end" (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco) and "We expect DSOs to trend back to the mid-60s over the course of the year" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Pipeline volatility and timing: "broader pipeline construction demand is still developing" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco) and "we are currently taking a conservative view around second half project timing and productivity" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco).

  • Communications margin drag from portfolio actions: "costs to exit certain markets in our DIRECTV fulfillment business" (Executive VP & CFO Dimarco), with CEO Mas adding, "We don't expect any more to continue throughout the year" regarding those costs.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as "the strongest first quarter in our history" (CEO Jose Mas) and raised full-year targets to "$17.5 billion" revenue, "$1.5 billion" adjusted EBITDA, and "$8.79" EPS (CEO Mas), while pointing to record backlog of "$20.3 billion" (CEO Mas). Across Q&A, executives repeatedly positioned 2026 guidance as conservative after the Q1 beat (CEO Mas), emphasized improving visibility in Power Delivery following Greenlink permitting progress (Executive VP & CFO Paul Dimarco), and framed data center-related construction and connectivity as a multi-year driver (CEO Mas).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Air Products raised at BMO, citing strong environment and execution
data.description:
Air Products and Chemicals (APD) up 0.4% in Friday's trading as BMO Capital upgraded shares to Outperform from Market Perform with a $360 price target, hiked from $325, saying the producer of gases and chemicals for industrial use is no longer a "show-me" story after management has delivered in a tough environment, driving costs down, winning new business, and cleaning house on most of its mega-projects.

With a number of tailwinds also likely to benefit Air Products (APD), such as new or higher pricing in the core, "less bad" helium pricing, and better volumes, BMO analyst John McNulty said the stock's risk/reward is compelling.

McNulty pointed to successful cost-cutting, a more rigorous approach to the investment in Darrow—if the company moves forward, the new capital has a greater likelihood of generating a solid return—and a partnership with Yara for green ammonia that helps reduce some of the risk around going it alone.

In addition to evidence of improved execution, the analyst noted opportunities that should help Air Products (APD) enjoy improved earnings tied to the current environment, including tight helium markets, inflation that should facilitate better pricing in the core, and higher refinery run rates.

data.title: Quaker Houghton outlines $20M-$30M 3-year cost program amid expected 200-300 bps Q2 gross margin pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quaker Houghton (KWR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Quaker Houghton opened the quarter pointing to share gains and volume growth despite weak end markets. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “We delivered a strong first quarter with organic volumes up 3% year-over-year,” and added that it was “our third consecutive quarter of adjusted EBITDA growth,” with performance “driven by new business wins in all regions” and “double-digit organic volume growth in Asia Pacific.”

  • Management tied the quarter to relative outperformance versus end markets and sequential margin improvement. CEO Joseph Berquist said Quaker Houghton “outperform[ed] our end markets, which we estimate were down approximately 1% in the quarter,” and added, “Gross margins improved from the fourth quarter, increasing 150 basis points sequentially and 40 basis points year-over-year,” citing “higher utilization of fixed assets and improved operational performance.”

  • The company’s key new strategic announcement was a transformation program targeted at structurally higher profitability, alongside manufacturing footprint actions. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “Today, we are announcing the launch of a new transformation program that will reduce cost and complexity across the organization, optimize our manufacturing network, strengthen sales and technical capabilities and simplify global processes,” and described it as “central to achieving adjusted EBITDA margins at or above our target of 18%.”

  • Management framed the Middle East conflict primarily as an input-cost and logistics risk with limited direct revenue exposure. CEO Joseph Berquist said 2025 sales to “North Africa and the Middle East” were “less than 2% of total company net sales,” but added, “we expect higher raw material and shipping costs in the second quarter,” noting pricing actions “across all regions.”

  • CFO Tom Coler summarized the quarter’s top-line and profitability drivers and highlighted the cadence of acquisition impacts. Tom Coler said, “First quarter net sales were $480 million,” and added, “Acquisitions contributed an additional 4% to net sales, primarily related to Dipsol, which will become part of our organic base beginning in Q2,” while gross margin “improved…to 36.8%, near the high end of our target range.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its macro view while flagging a near-term margin dip tied to inflationary inputs. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “we still continue to predict flat end market conditions for the full year,” and added, “we expect sequential volume and revenue growth in Q2,” while also expecting “temporary gross margin pressure” from higher input costs.

  • The most specific near-term guidepost was gross margin compression in Q2 with a recovery path by year-end. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “Our current estimate is that second quarter gross margins will be 200 to 300 basis points below quarter 1 on a sequential basis,” and added, “we expect to fully recover gross margins within our target range of 36% to 37% as we exit the year.”

  • The transformation program was positioned with staged savings targets. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “We expect to exit this year with approximately $10 million in new run rate savings,” and added, “Over the next 3 years, we see a clear path to delivering at least $20 million to $30 million of sustainable structural cost improvement.”

Financial Results

  • Quaker Houghton reported first-quarter net sales of $480 million, adjusted EBITDA of $73 million, and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.63. CFO Tom Coler said, “We delivered $73 million of adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter,” and added, “our GAAP diluted earnings per share were $1.13 and our non-GAAP diluted earnings per share were $1.63.”

  • Segment performance was led by Asia Pacific, while the Americas showed softer earnings. CFO Tom Coler said Asia Pacific sales “increased 25% year-over-year,” with “organic volume growth of 10%,” and added, “Segment earnings in Asia Pacific increased approximately $8 million or 32%,” while “Segment earnings in the Americas decreased approximately $5 million or 8%.”

  • Cash flow and capital deployment were described as seasonally low in Q1 and weighed against major projects and capital allocation priorities. CFO Tom Coler said, “Cash generated from operations was $4 million in the first quarter,” “Capital expenditures in the first quarter were approximately $11 million,” and the company still expects “full year 2026 capital expenditures to be approximately 2.5% to 3.5% of sales.”

  • The company emphasized refinancing-driven liquidity and flexibility. CFO Tom Coler said the amended credit agreement “extended our nearest debt maturity…to April 2031,” and added it increased revolver availability “by approximately $300 million,” with an option to increase it “by approximately $331 million for additional liquidity.”

Q&A

  • Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners: Asked for detail on raw-material inflation (base oils/additives/oleochemicals) and availability; CEO Joseph Berquist said, “all 3 of those buckets are higher,” and added, “we’ve been very fortunate to not have any issues” on supply, while reiterating the pricing lag driven by contracts and “index agreements…adjust on a quarterly basis.”

  • Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners: Asked what’s new in the transformation program versus prior initiatives; CEO Joseph Berquist said, “This is not a reaction to what’s happening in the Middle East,” and cited “spans in layers,” “redundancy,” and post-2019 integration complexity, adding, “our master data is a little messy.”

  • Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners: Asked whether Q2 EBITDA could be similar to Q1 given margin pressure; CEO Joseph Berquist responded, “Mike, I think that’s fair,” and added, “we have a couple of parts of our business where we’re actually adding labor…to keep up with demand.”

  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Asked whether the expanded credit facility was for growth, buybacks, or M&A; CFO Tom Coler said it was “first and foremost…about an opportunity to extend maturities,” and CEO Joseph Berquist added “yes to both” regarding buybacks and M&A, saying, “the M&A pipeline…remains active.”

  • Daniel Rizzo, Jefferies: Asked about the new plan’s cash costs; CEO Joseph Berquist said, “we didn’t mention anything specific about the cash cost,” adding, “we’re not planning to put a big new ERP system in,” and suggested, “what’s typical here is 1 to 1.5x to achieve the types of synergies that we’re looking at.”

  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC Capital Markets: Pressed on margin recapture and demand resilience; CEO Joseph Berquist said, “the goal overall is we have these target gross margin ranges,” and added, “The demand environment right now, surprisingly is very strong.”

  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities (follow-up): Asked about potential demand disruption if conflict extends; CEO Joseph Berquist said, “there’s an equal chance that this thing is prolonged or not prolonged,” and added, “we’re not seeing that sort of catastrophic demand disruption on the horizon.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative, centered on inflation pass-through mechanics and downside scenarios, including explicit questions on “margin pressure,” “demand destruction,” and whether transformation costs could be quantified.

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A; CEO Joseph Berquist used confidence language on recovery timing (“we expect to recover margins within 1 to 2 quarters”) while repeatedly highlighting uncertainty and lag dynamics tied to index pricing and rapid cost moves.

  • Versus last quarter: The tone shifted from Q4’s “stabiliz[ing]” raw materials to Q1’s inflationary spike and explicit near-term margin compression, with more detailed discussion of geopolitical-driven costs and contingency framing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The largest change in messaging was the move from Q4’s expectation that “Raw material costs are expected to remain steady in the first part of the year” to Q1’s expectation of “higher raw material and shipping costs in the second quarter,” plus quantified gross margin pressure.

  • Strategic emphasis escalated from Q4’s continued “complexity and cost reduction plan” to a newly launched “transformation program,” including explicit multi-year savings targets and a stated objective to push adjusted EBITDA margins “above 18% and pushing 20% eventually.”

  • Capital structure discussion intensified in Q1 with the amended credit agreement extending maturities and increasing revolver capacity; Q4 discussion focused more on leverage reduction and shareholder returns.

Risks and Concerns

  • The principal near-term risk highlighted was input-cost inflation and logistics disruption tied to the Middle East conflict. CEO Joseph Berquist said, “we anticipate temporary gross margin pressure,” and noted a “typical lag between rising costs and price realization.”

  • Management also cited regional demand headwinds, including “weak automotive production,” and in the Americas “a lingering customer outage, tariff uncertainty and weather-related disruptions,” while emphasizing pricing actions and operational flexibility as mitigations.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as another quarter of share-driven growth and sequential margin improvement, while shifting investor focus to a near-term gross margin step-down in Q2 (200 to 300 basis points sequentially) from rapid raw-material inflation. The call’s main new catalyst was the launch of a transformation program tied to an adjusted EBITDA margin target of at least 18%, with expectations to exit 2026 with about $10 million of new run-rate savings and a stated path to $20 million to $30 million of structural cost improvement over three years, alongside ongoing network actions in Germany and China and added financing flexibility through the amended credit agreement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ares targets 16%-20% FRE CAGR in 2026 while deploying $158B of available capital
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ares Management Corporation (ARES) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Our AUM increased 18% year-over-year to $644 billion, and our fee-paying AUM increased 19% to $400 billion.” (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Michael Arougheti)

  • “We are on track for another record year of fundraising as we raised $30 billion of gross capital in Q1, which is our highest ever first quarter and that's up 46% compared to last year's record first quarter.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Our available capital continues to expand... and it now stands at over $158 billion.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Within U.S. direct lending, equity flows into our non-traded BDC have moderated relative to prior periods, while fund performance and underlying credit fundamentals remain strong.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Quarterly management fees exceeded $1 billion for the first time in our firm's history and increased 22% compared to the prior year period.” (Partner & CFO Jarrod Phillips)

  • “Fee-related earnings were $464 million in the quarter, increasing 26% year-over-year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We held the final close for ASOF III... raising over $8.3 billion of equity commitments and nearly $10 billion, including related transaction vehicles.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We expect to complete the fundraise in the second quarter at its hard cap as the fund is already meaningfully oversubscribed.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We've sized the third-party market opportunity at around $900 billion” for data centers. (Partner & Co-President Blair Jacobson)

Outlook

  • “As we look ahead, we remain confident that we're on track to meet our financial objectives for the year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “In conclusion, for the year 2026, we're on track with our longer-term goals of generating compound annual growth of 16% to 20% in FRE, 20% to 25% in realized income and 20% in dividends.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We anticipate continued FRE margin expansion, and we expect to be within the upper end of our 0 to 150 basis point annual target this year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We also have some exciting structural enhancements to our main fund series... The fourth vintage in the series will be a fully levered fund and we plan to launch a new unlevered evergreen U.S. senior direct lending core product.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We anticipate a first close late third quarter or early fourth quarter of this year.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Yes, we have not changed our guidance.” (CEO Arougheti)

Financial results

  • “FRE margin expanded 90 basis points year-over-year to 42.4%.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We generated $75 million in realized net performance income, an 84% increase over the year ago period.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Realized income for the quarter was $503 million, representing growth of 24% year-over-year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “After-tax realized income per share was $1.24, up 14% compared to the prior year period.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Fee-related performance revenues totaled $20 million in the quarter, which were driven by APMF.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Our tax rate in the quarter totaled 13.5%, just above the midpoint of our 11% to 15% expected range for the year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Over the last 12 months, we generated time-weighted returns of approximately 12% to 15% in U.S. direct lending strategies... and over 20% in APAC credit.” (CFO Phillips)

Q&A

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: Asked about “evolving demand dynamics” across institutional, insurance, and retail private credit as retail funds decelerate; Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti: “If we originate a senior secured loan... each of those three pools will get to participate,” and “if you are beginning to see slowing inflows or increased redemptions... those assets will find their ways into other funds and therefore, will not have an impact on our profitability.”

  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked where deployment is picking up and whether non-traded BDCs were the “incremental buyer”; CEO Arougheti: “We saw a little bit of a slowdown in the U.S. direct lending part of the business,” but called out strength in “infra and real estate... European direct lending... Secondaries and structured solutions,” adding “the pipeline is reengaged” in U.S. direct lending.

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Asked about wealth appetite outside credit and confidence in the “$125 billion” target; CEO Arougheti: “While the U.S. private credit funds are seeing slowing demand, we're seeing increasing demand elsewhere,” and on the target, “Yes, we have not changed our guidance.”

  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous: Pressed on timing for direct lending “shadow pipeline” to show in “hard numbers”; CEO Arougheti: “The aggregate pipeline across the firm is at a record level and that the direct lending pipeline is increasing in momentum,” citing prior “pause” dynamics and adding “I can't guarantee that that's the case.”

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Asked about realizations cadence and 2027 margin implications; Partner & CFO Phillips: “The hardest thing for us is to pick the exact quarter,” and on margins, “we expect to still be well within that 0 to 150 guidance.”

  • Bart Dziarski, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about secondaries acceleration; CEO Arougheti: “The GP-led part of the market represents half, if not more, of the current deployment opportunity,” and said the segment looks “least well-capitalized” with “about a one-to-one relationship” between annual deployment and industry dry powder.

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about Europe direct lending deployment; CEO Arougheti: “The deployment there has been quite robust,” adding “pipelines in Europe are as healthy as they are here.”

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about forward-looking software stress testing and positioning; CEO Arougheti: “This is going to play out slowly over time,” and said Ares is “very confident in the quality of the software book,” while also “exit[ing] some names where maybe we have less conviction.”

  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Asked for more detail on data centers and total market size; Partner & Co-President Jacobson: “We've been investing in the digital space broadly for the past 10 or 15 years,” and described a “multitrillion dollar market opportunity,” while CEO Arougheti added it is also about “GPUs, it's power and energy.”

  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked if institutional demand differs by geography; CEO Arougheti: “It's pretty broad-based,” while highlighting “a consolidation theme” where large institutions are “doing more with fewer GP partners.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on retail/wealth flows, software/AI risk, and timing of direct lending reacceleration, including “you can't really see that in the hard numbers” (Patrick Davitt, Autonomous) and requests to “fine tune” guidance (Benjamin Budish, Barclays).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, with repeated emphasis on resilience and diversification, including “we're excited and confident about the opportunities ahead” (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti) and “we remain confident that we're on track” (Partner & CFO Phillips).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management again leaned into confidence on credit fundamentals and software exposure; in Q4 2025, CEO Arougheti said “we see no change to our earnings growth outlook from AI risks,” while in Q1 2026 he added third-party work concluding “86% of the portfolio with low risk of potential AI disruption.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized fundraising momentum and record first-quarter fundraising (“$30 billion of gross capital in Q1”), while Q4 2025 emphasized full-year records (“record $113 billion in total fundraising for the year”). (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti)

  • Q1 2026 added more specificity on wealth redemption mechanics and potential firmwide impact, including “it could impact our FPAUM by approximately 1% annually” under a stated stress scenario. (CEO Arougheti)

  • Q4 2025 framed 2026 as “our most significant year yet for the realization of some of our European-style performance fees,” while Q1 2026 commentary shifted to seasonality and cadence, with CFO Phillips emphasizing quarter selection difficulty: “the hardest thing for us is to pick the exact quarter.”

  • Q1 2026 broadened data-center market framing via the GCP/Ada capability and market sizing (“around $900 billion” third-party opportunity), building on Q4 2025’s view that “digital infrastructure [will] be a key contributor to our business in 2026 and beyond.” (Partner & CFO Phillips; Partner & Co-President Jacobson)

Risks and concerns

  • “Heightened geopolitical issues” and “the Iran War” were cited as amplifying a seasonal slowdown and contributing to a slower U.S. direct lending transaction environment. (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti)

  • Retail vehicle flow volatility remained a focal point, with management stressing structural liquidity design: “these vehicles are specifically designed to align liquidity with the underlying assets.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • AI/software disruption risk was addressed with portfolio segmentation and third-party review, concluding only “1% of the portfolio was categorized as having high risk of AI disruption.” (CEO Arougheti)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of record fundraising and scaling profitability, highlighted by $644 billion in AUM, more than $158 billion of available capital, and quarterly management fees exceeding $1 billion for the first time. Executives emphasized institutional fundraising durability, expanding deployment pipelines (especially outside slower U.S. direct lending early in the quarter), and continued confidence in 2026 objectives, while using detailed disclosures to frame retail redemption mechanics and AI/software exposure as manageable within Ares’ diversified platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Jobs report, Disney drama, Citi event, and Mickey D's crafted beverage push
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - May 4

 

  • Volatility watch - Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include OneConstruction Group (ONEG), Vistance Networks (VISN), and Bandwidth (BAND). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Oio Group (OIO), Option Care Health (OPCH), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). 
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Palantir Technologies (PLTR), On Semiconductor (ON), Tyson Foods (TSN), and Pinterest (PINS). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Firefly Aerospace (FLY) after it reports.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming during the week include Blackstone (BX), Citigroup (C), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Energy Transfer (ET).
  • IPO watch - Rare Earths Americas (REA) is expected to price its IPOs and begin trading. The IPO lockup periods expire on blocks of shares of Beta Technologies (BETA) and Exzeo Group (XZO).
  • All week - The China Labor Day break is expected to provide a boost to Macau casino stocks such as Wynn Macau (WYNMF) (WYNMY), Sands China (SCHYY) (SCHYF), MGM China (MCHVF) (MCHVY), Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), SJM Holdings (SJMHF) (SJMHY), Melco Resorts & Entertainment (MLCO), and Studio City International (MSC).
  • All day - The Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices will be released. The SLOOS report will provide an update on the conditions, supply, and demand of bank loans extended to customers in the U.S. Tightening standards can signal stress in the banking system or broader economy, impacting financial stocks.
  • All day - IBM's (IBM) four-day IBM Think will begin. The flagship enterprise tech conference will be focused on agentic AI, with sessions and demos focused on how companies can redesign operations with AI at the core to drive ROI, productivity, and growth.
  • All day - The three-day Milken Global Conference will include panels featuring the CEOs from Gap (GAP), Constellation Energy (CEG), and PayPal (PYPL).
  • All day - Shareholders with both Devon Energy (DVN) and Coterra Energy (CTRA) will vote on the pending merger between the companies.

 

Tuesday - May 5

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include AMD (AMD), HSBC (HSBC), Arista Networks (ANET), Shopify (SHOP), Pfizer (PFE), Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Energy Transfer (ER), and Duke Energy (DUK). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Lucid Group (LCID) and Joby Aviation (JOBY) after they report.
  • Fedspeak - Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman will speak at the Washington Financial Symposium, and Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr is scheduled to speak on banking regulation.
  • All day - American Express (AXP) will hold its annual shareholder meeting.
  • All day - The three-day CoinDesk Consensus conference will begin. Notable speakers during the event include Binance founder Changpeng Zhao, Strategy (MSTR) Executive Chairman Michael Saylor, and Cloudflare (NET) Chief Strategy Officer Stephanie Cohen.
  • 10:00 a.m. The New Home Sales report will be released by the Census Bureau.

 

Wednesday - May 6

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Arm Holdings (ARM), Disney (DIS), Uber Technologies (UBER), DoorDash (DASH), AppLovin (APP), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), and Novo Nordisk (NVO). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Beyond Meat (BYND) and Fastly (FSLY) after their reports are released.
  • Fedspeak - Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago President Austan Goolsbee and St. Louis President Alberto Musalem are scheduled to speak at separate events.
  • Fedspeak - Bloomberg is expected to publish its OPEC production survey for April.
  • All day - Corning (GLW) will hold an event for investors and analysts at the New York Stock Exchange.
  • All day - The window for Boeing (BA) and Airbus (EADSF) to issue their monthly deliveries reports opens.
  • All day - Shareholders with Penumbra (PEN) vote on the $14.5B buyout offer from Boston Scientific (BSX). 
  • All day - McDonald's (MCD) will add six new crafted beverages to its menu, including three specialized refreshers and three crafted sodas. The new crafted beverage lineup from the nation's largest restaurant chain will be closely watched by rivals such as Starbucks (SBUX), Taco Bell (YUM), Dutch Bros. (BROS), and Dunkin' looking to protect their market share.
  • All day - Anthropic (ANTHRO) is holding a Code with Claude event in San Francisco to highlight new Claude capabilities.
  • 8:30 a.m. Disney (DIS) will hold its earnings conference call. Analysts will be focused on the update on the FCC investigation of ABC, streaming momentum, ESPN/NFL, theme park trends, broader content/AI strategy, and capital allocation plans.
  • Postmarket - Costco (COST) will release its monthly sales report.

 

Thursday - May 7

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Shell (SHEL), McDonald's (MCD), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Airbnb (ABNB), and Coreweave (CRWV). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Coinbase Global (COIN), Rocket Lab (RKLB), and Unity Software (U) after they report.
  • All day - Citi (C) will hold its Investor Day. The event is expected to be a strategy-and-guidance reset for the "post-transformation" bank, with management likely laying out a clearer long-term roadmap for returns, capital deployment, and growth priorities.
  • All day - The Carvana (CVNA) 5-for-1 stock split will become effective assuming that shareholders approve it at the May 5 meeting.
  • All day - The Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index will be released.
  • 8:30 a.m. McDonald's (MCD) will hold its earnings conference call. Lamb Weston (LW) is a major supplier to McDonald's that sees its share price correlate tightly with it on the day earnings are released.

 

Friday - May 8

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Toyota Motor (TM), Enbridge (ENB), Sony (SONY), and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).
  • Fedspeak - The Hoover Institution monetary policy panel will feature Fed speakers Michelle Bowman, Mary Daly, Austan Goolsbee, and Christopher Waller.
  • All day - Shareholders with both Sun Country Airlines (SNCY) and Allegiant Travel (ALGT) will vote on the pending merger between the companies.
  • 8:30 a.m. The March jobs report will be released. Economists expect 49K job additions for the month and for the unemployment rate to remain at 4.3%.

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this article.

data.title: Lazard outlines $500M 2027 private capital advisory revenue target as it plans Lazard CL launch
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lazard (LAZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Before turning to our first quarter results and outlook for the year, I want to start with our announcement of the acquisition of Campbell Lutyens, and the future establishment of Lazard CL, a new private capital advisory unit within Lazard that will serve as our third global business" (CEO & Chairman Peter Orszag).

  • "Along with our existing PCA group, the transaction combines 2 highly complementary advisory platforms that will create the leading primary and secondary advisory business globally, with approximately $500 million in anticipated combined 2027 revenue" (CEO & Chairman Orszag).

  • "Firm-wide adjusted net revenue was $673 million, up 5% compared to 1 year ago" (CEO & Chairman Orszag). He said Financial Advisory had "several transactions move to later in the year" and cited "robust growth in restructuring and liability management, and Private Capital Advisory along with solid M&A performance in Europe" as offsets.

  • "In Asset Management, we delivered net inflows of $9 billion this quarter, the highest level of quarterly net flow in almost 20 years" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), and added that "we continue to believe 2026 will be a year in which investors increasingly reallocate towards emerging and international markets."

  • "Financial Advisory adjusted net revenue was $356 million for the first quarter of 2026, 4% lower than the prior year" (Chief Financial Officer Tracy Farr).

  • "Adjusted net revenue was $309 million for the first quarter of 2026, up 17% from the prior year quarter" (CFO Farr), adding "as of March 31, we reported AUM $259 billion" and noting an Edgewater-related "$78 million noncash gain" in GAAP results that was excluded from adjusted results.

Outlook

  • "We anticipate the transaction closing before the end of the calendar year" (CEO & Chairman Orszag) regarding Campbell Lutyens.

  • "The acquisition is expected to be EPS accretive in 2027 with no synergies assumed" (CFO Farr).

  • "I think we would still guide you closer to a comp ratio for the full year similar to what we had last year, around 65.5%" (CFO Farr), while adding that the company expects its 2026 effective tax rate "to be in the high 20s percent range."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that Lazard would "anticipate hiring within or above our stated range going forward," management said for 2026, "I think that we will be within our range this year in terms of net adds rather than above it" (CEO & Chairman Orszag).

Financial Results

  • "Revenues reflected management fees of $296 million for the first quarter, 25% higher than the first quarter of 2025, and up 3% on a sequential basis" (CFO Farr), while "incentive fees totaled $11 million" and "average AUM for the first quarter was $266 billion."

  • "During the quarter, we had net inflows of $9 billion, market appreciation of $354 million, foreign exchange depreciation of $3 billion and divestitures of $1.5 billion" (CFO Farr).

  • "Our adjusted compensation expense was $471 million for the first quarter of 2026, resulting in a compensation ratio of 69.9%" (CFO Farr), while "our adjusted non-comp expense was $149 million... a non-compensation ratio of 22.1%."

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we returned $174 million to shareholders" (CFO Farr) and "yesterday, we declared a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share."

Q&A

  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about full-year comp ratio and operating leverage; CFO Farr: "we would still guide you closer to a comp ratio for the full year... around 65.5%" and said Lazard has launched "a long-dated program" on support-function efficiencies with "more details... in the second half of the year."

  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about Campbell Lutyens network effects; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "we believe... we're pro forma the leader" and described "a network effect, a flywheel effect in both directions" plus a combined "data-rich environment" paired with AI.

  • Alexander Bond, KBW: Asked about Campbell Lutyens mix and white space; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said there was "very little overlap" and highlighted complementary secondaries ("GP transactions" vs. "LP transactions"), asset classes, and geography.

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about sponsor M&A recovery timing; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said private equity deal activity has a "waiting for Godot" element and added that connectivity to private capital extends beyond PE M&A.

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Asset Management flows and fee rate; CEO Lazard Asset Management Christopher Hogbin: "I would not straight line the number from Q1 through the year" and flagged possible "moderation" but said Lazard remains confident on "net inflows for the full year" and that the management fee rate "should stay around this level through the remainder of the year."

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about deal price and financing; CFO Farr: detailed "$575 million total noncontingent consideration" with "$460 million" upfront and an "$85 million of performance-based earnout," with settlement optionality in "either a cash-like security or stock."

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about acquired economics; CFO Farr: said Campbell Lutyens had "healthy operating margins in the mid-20s percent range" and discussed the acquisition multiple relative to Lazard.

  • Ryan Kenny, Morgan Stanley: Asked whether integration rules out more M&A; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said Lazard will remain "quite disciplined," did not expect traditional asset management acquisitions, and said it is "taking a very active look" at wealth management.

  • Ryan Kenny, Morgan Stanley: Asked about proposed European merger rules; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said "it could be" meaningful and linked the change to improving European growth dynamics.

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about pipeline conversion and slipped deals; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "this is not a quarterly business" and declined "false precision" on timing, while cautioning there is "no guarantee" conflict clearances convert to revenue.

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about synergies; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said 2027 accretion assumed no cost synergies but "I have confidence that there will" be synergies, and CFO Farr pointed to potential "shared service" and real estate efficiencies.

  • Daniel Cocchiara, BofA Securities: Asked how Middle East conflict impacted Asset Management; CEO Hogbin: said Lazard "didn't really see any impact in the flows" and noted institutional investors are "longer term."

  • Brendan O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Asked about private credit stress and timing; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said challenges are "concentrated in the software sector" and more severe where retail money is involved; CFO Farr said Capital Solutions can perform better with "bespoke credit" needs.

  • Brendan O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Asked about cross-border M&A and supply chains; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "large multinational firms are definitively rethinking their supply chain footprint" and described a "new environment" of geopolitical choke points.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on compensation leverage, deal pricing/multiples, sponsor M&A softness, and private credit stress (e.g., questions on "price paid" and whether comp can improve).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and expansion-oriented around Lazard CL and Asset Management momentum, while Q&A responses were more cautious on timing and variability (e.g., "this is not a quarterly business" (CEO & Chairman Orszag) and "I would not straight line the number from Q1 through the year" (CEO Hogbin)).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more internally focused transition messaging (CFO handoff) and broad 2026 growth optimism, this call centered on a major acquisition and included more mechanical detail on consideration structure and more explicit caution about quarter-to-quarter advisory lumpiness.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The current call introduced the Campbell Lutyens acquisition and Lazard CL, including the stated "approximately $500 million" combined 2027 revenue outlook and an expectation the deal will close "before the end of the calendar year" (CEO & Chairman Orszag); the prior call emphasized Lazard 2030 execution, MD productivity targets, and leadership changes in finance and Asset Management.

  • Hiring language shifted from the prior quarter’s expectation to be "within or above" the 10–15 net MD addition range to "within our range" for 2026 (CEO & Chairman Orszag), alongside discussion that about "40%" of MDs remain in a ramping period (CFO Farr).

  • Asset Management narrative moved from Q4’s focus on overcoming a large sub-advised outflow to Q1’s emphasis on $9 billion net inflows and fee rate stability, with management explicitly warning against annualizing Q1 flows.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Geopolitical uncertainty" and "the path forward in the Middle East" were cited as conditions influencing the advisory environment (CEO & Chairman Orszag), with management also highlighting the fragility of "choke points" and supply chain reconfiguration.

  • Advisory execution risk was framed through timing variability: "Financial Advisory activity can admittedly be uneven from quarter-to-quarter" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), and management acknowledged that conflict clearances do not guarantee announced deals or revenue.

  • Private credit stress risk was discussed as more sector-specific: "the private credit challenges are concentrated in the software sector" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), with management pointing to restructuring, liability management, secondaries, and capital solutions as areas of activity if complexities increase.

Final Takeaway

Lazard’s management positioned the Campbell Lutyens acquisition and the planned Lazard CL unit as a step-change in private capital connectivity, highlighted by an anticipated $500 million combined 2027 revenue profile and an expectation of EPS accretion in 2027 without assumed synergies, while reiterating that advisory revenues can be lumpy quarter to quarter. Management paired that strategic expansion with cost-discipline messaging (including a full-year comp ratio guide around 65.5%) and pointed to Asset Management momentum—tempered by a warning not to extrapolate Q1 flows—as it framed Lazard 2030 as increasingly supported by diversified advisory levers and active-management demand for global and emerging market exposure.
